attributes.stem:
An 83-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset, severe right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking. He has a medical history of hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type 2 diabetes, and mild cognitive impairment. His medications include amlodipine, rosuvastatin, metformin, gliclazide and vitamin D. He lives alone and was fully independent prior to admission.

He is triaged as a stroke call in the emergency department and undergoes an urgent CT brain, angiography and perfusion scan which identifies a left middle cerebral artery stroke. He is subsequently treated successfully with intravenous thrombolysis.

On examination three days following his initial presentation, his pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 160/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He has mild right upper and lower limb weakness. There are no signs of dysphasia or dysarthria. Cognitive testing with the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) shows a score of 24/30, losing points for delayed recall and attention. He is being considered for transfer to an inpatient rehabilitation unit.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following factors will most impact the likelihood of successful rehabilitation in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the patient's cognitive function is likely to have the most significant impact on his rehabilitation success. While all the factors mentioned can influence rehabilitation outcomes, cognitive status is particularly crucial for engaging in and benefiting from rehabilitation therapies.

The patient's history of mild cognitive impairment is a key consideration. Cognitive function affects a patient's ability to understand and follow instructions, learn new compensatory strategies, and actively participate in therapy sessions. Patients with better cognitive function tend to have more successful rehabilitation outcomes as they can more effectively engage in the rehabilitation process. It's also important to note that mood disorders can further impact cognitive function, and appropriate recognition and treatment of post-stroke mood changes is an essential component of optimising cognitive performance during rehabilitation.

Age alone is not a contraindication for stroke rehabilitation. While advanced age can affect the rate and extent of recovery, many older adults can still make significant functional gains with appropriate rehabilitation.

The patient's living situation (living alone, previously independent) is relevant for discharge planning and may influence the goals of rehabilitation, but it is not typically the most crucial factor in determining the success of the rehabilitation process itself.

Premorbid functional status is an important consideration in rehabilitation planning and can influence outcomes. This patient was fully independent before the stroke, suggesting good baseline function. Research has shown that certain premorbid features such as excess comorbidities, smoking, sleep disorders, obesity, and anxious personality traits can negatively impact rehabilitation outcomes. However, cognitive function more directly impacts the patient's ability to participate in and benefit from rehabilitation interventions in this particular patient.

Stroke severity certainly influences rehabilitation outcomes, but in this case, the patient has only mild residual weakness, suggesting a relatively less severe stroke. This factor, while important, may not be as critical as cognitive function in determining rehabilitation success.

In summary, while all these factors play a role in rehabilitation outcomes, cognitive function is often the most critical determinant of a patient's ability to engage in and benefit from stroke rehabilitation. This underscores the importance of cognitive assessment and tailoring rehabilitation strategies to the patient's cognitive capabilities, while also considering the impact of premorbid features on recovery potential.
learningPoints: Cognitive function is the most critical determinant of stroke rehabilitation success, affecting a patient's ability to engage with and benefit from therapy.
Premorbid factors including comorbidities, smoking, sleep disorders, obesity, and anxious personality traits negatively impact stroke rehabilitation outcomes.
references: Li S. Stroke Recovery Is a Journey: Prediction and Potentials of Motor Recovery after a Stroke from a Practical Perspective. Life. 2023; 13(10):2061. https://doi.org/10.3390/life13102061

Stolwyk, Rene J., Mihaljcic, Tijana, Wong, Dana K., Chapman, Jodie E. and Rogers, Jeffrey M.. (2021). Poststroke cognitive impairment negatively impacts activity and participation outcomes : A systematic review and meta-analysis. Stroke. 52(2), pp. 748-760. https://doi.org/10.1161/STROKEAHA.120.032215

Karaahmet OZ, Umay E, Gurcay E, Serçe A, Gundogdu I, Cakci A. The effect of premorbid features on post-stroke rehabilitation outcome. Iran J Neurol. 2018 Jan 5;17(1):38-46. PMID: 30186558; PMCID: PMC6121201. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6121201/
